metadata.title: Cross-sectional analysis of the bond/credit, bank, and funding-graph cross-sections (data behind docs/charts.html#xsec)
metadata.frame: Cross-sectional (not time-series) analysis across four cross-sections, in the order requested: (1) bond/credit, (2) banks, (3) funding-graph cross-layer connectors, (4) a unified normalized snapshot. For each, the standard quant-credit toolkit is computed: dispersion over time, relative-value z-scores/percentiles, latest-date snapshot ranking, and cross-sectional correlation + PCA first-principal-component (PC1) share. The PC1 share on monthly spread changes is the empirical 'common factor' signature; a high value means the segments move as one and cross-sectional diversification is illusory at the system level.
metadata.engine: models/graph/cross_section.py -> data/cross_section.json; rendered by build_charts.py (docs/charts.html#xsec) and build_dashboard.py (dashboard #xsec)

methods.dispersion_over_time: cross-sectional std / range across segments each period; compresses in calm regimes and widens in stress (credit-dispersion-as-stress gauge).
methods.relative_value_zscore: each segment's latest level vs its OWN trailing history (z-score + percentile); standard desk relative value.
methods.snapshot_ranking: latest period, every segment ranked side by side.
methods.cross_sectional_correlation_pca: Pearson correlation of monthly CHANGES + PC1 variance share via power iteration on the correlation matrix (eigenvalues of a correlation matrix sum to n, so PC1 share = lambda1 / n).

literature.common_factor: Collin-Dufresne, Goldstein & Martin (2001), 'The Determinants of Credit Spread Changes', Journal of Finance 56(6): monthly credit-spread changes are dominated by a single common (systematic) factor that standard structural variables do not explain. A high PC1 share on Delta-OAS is that factor.
literature.dispersion_stress: Cross-sectional credit-spread dispersion as a market-stress / credit-cycle gauge (compresses in complacency, blows out in distress).
literature.sovereign_fragmentation: Sovereign-yield dispersion across a currency/region bloc as a fragmentation gauge (euro-area periphery literature).
literature.relative_value: z-score / percentile vs own history as desk-standard relative value.

stress_test_factor_reversal.summary: What the ~91% PC1 common factor REVERSING does - the asymmetric stress case (#49).
stress_test_factor_reversal.diversification_is_illusory_quantified: For n equal-weight segments with average pairwise correlation rho, portfolio variance / single-name variance = 1/n + (1-1/n)*rho. US credit ladder (n=5, rho=0.88) => 0.904: holding all five rating buckets removes only ~9.6% of variance (vs ~80% for a zero-correlation 5-name basket); effective independent bets ~= 1/0.904 ~= 1.1. On a reversal there is almost no cushion - AAA->BBB->CCC widen together.
stress_test_factor_reversal.compression_is_the_pre_stress_tell: Dispersion compresses in calm, blows out in stress. Municipal and TRACE-breadth read COMPRESSED now while US credit reads ELEVATED - the highest-PC1 segments are priced most for calm; compressed dispersion historically PRECEDES the blow-out. The cross-section is primed, not relieved.
stress_test_factor_reversal.reversal_is_the_self_marked_forcing_even: self_marked_value U3 proves the four self-marked gaps (bank HTM, AI fair-value marks, private-credit NAV, insurance captives) co-move under a common factor and cannot be netted; a PC1 credit reversal is that factor turning, pricing all four at once - no asset-class diversification either.
stress_test_factor_reversal.honest_scope: The LEVEL of stress on a reversal is not forecastable (date unknowable, spec-unwind-timing); the math fixes the SHAPE - a one-factor reversal is undiversified across both the credit cross-section (~1.1 effective bets) and the four self-marked asset classes (U3), so the loss is correlated, simultaneous, and un-hedgeable by holding 'many different credits.'
stress_test_factor_reversal.grade: fact (rho=0.88 / PC1=91% inputs + the diversification arithmetic) + labeled interpretation (the reversal scenario)

disagreement_with_literature: Mean-variance portfolio theory assumes that holding many different credits diversifies idiosyncratic risk away. When PC1 explains most of the cross-sectional variance (segments move as one), that diversification is illusory at the system level. The measured US-credit PC1 share (~91%) and average pairwise correlation (~0.88) on monthly OAS changes are the empirical agreement with the project's self-marked-value claim (models/z3/self_marked_value.py U1-U4: the gaps correlate under a common factor; there is no netting; carrying value is forced to converge on a forcing event). Reported, not hidden.

banks.metric: composite peer-relative z on HTM-hole / uninsured % / CRE-Tier1

graph_connectors.metric: bridge_score = z(degree) + z(neighbor-sectors) + 0.5 if both layers

key_results_2026_06_10.unified: every time-series segment z-scored vs its own history, read side by side; positive z = wide/cheap vs own history.
key_results_2026_06_10.note: Recomputed every run from data/fred_monthly.json, data/yahoo_monthly.json, data/tape_trace.json, data/bank_exposure.json, data/graph.json; values above are the 2026-06-10 snapshot.

data_inputs: data/fred_monthly.json (ICE BofA OAS rating ladder; developed sovereign 10Y; from FRED)
data/yahoo_monthly.json (per-state/quality muni ETF distribution yields; from the Yahoo chart API)
data/tape_trace.json (FINRA TRACE corporate breadth by quality tier)

sources.name: Collin-Dufresne, Goldstein & Martin (2001), The Determinants of Credit Spread Changes, Journal of Finance
FRED — ICE BofA US Corporate Index OAS (rating ladder)
FRED — ICE BofA US High Yield Index OAS
FRED — Long-term government bond yields, 10-year (OECD, by country)
FINRA — Fixed Income (TRACE) market data API
